treks.name: Girgenti-Laferla Cross
treks.shortDescription: Walking up to the Laferla Cross

checkpoints.checkpointText: The statue you just passed by marks the official start of the Pilgrims Way and represents the first station of a life-size open-air Via Crucis (Way of the Cross). Sculpted from local limestone over 125 years ago, it was originally part of a more ambitious plan by Reverend Paul Laferla to have a full set of processional figures. On Maundy Thursday, the path beginning at this statue is transformed into a glowing trail lit by hundreds of fjakkoli (traditional torches), leading pilgrims on a steep 200-meter climb to the summit.

This 16-meter landmark sits atop one of Malta's highest hills, but its current form is actually a 1963 Italian replica reinforced with bronze after the original 1903 iron cross was toppled by fierce gales. Hidden inside its massive limestone pedestal is a tiny, functioning chapel housing a rudimentary altar and three religious paintings, including a depiction of the Virgin Mary.

This stubborn chapel has been rebuilt three times since the 1400s after being repeatedly decimated by earthquakes, including a massive tremor in 1856 that leveled the structure. It sits directly on a geological fault line, which caused the building to start sliding down the hill; to save it, modern engineers had to anchor it to the plateau’s core using steel rods drilled 30 meters deep into the rock. Historically, it served as a vital maritime lighthouse of sorts, as its high elevation made it one of the first landmarks visible to sailors approaching Malta from the southwest.
As you descend back along this scenic road heading towards Dingli cliffs, take a moment to enjoy the panoramic view onto the Girgenti valley.

Looking down into this fertile valley, you are seeing one of the few places in Malta where water flows year-round thanks to the Għajn il-Kbira spring, which once powered multiple flour mills in the area. The dense greenery below isn't just scenic; its a managed ecosystem where citrus groves and pomegranate trees thrive in a unique microclimate that is significantly more humid than the rest of the island. During the era of the Knights, this valley was a highly guarded resource, serving as the private garden that supplied the Inquisitors with fresh produce and ice-cooled water during the sweltering summer months.

This open-air shrine was established in 1986 following the reported visions of local resident Ġuża Mifsud, who claimed the Virgin Mary chose this peaceful valley as a place of refuge. The site is uniquely inclusive, featuring a statue of the Suffering Christ funded by a Welsh couple and a statue of St. Michael based on a specific vision Ġuża had of the archangel.

Hope you enjoyed walking along the lovely cobbled downhill path to this small bridge in the valley bed. In a short moment we will take the final short stretch to the end point along a busy vehicular road. Cross safely to the left side of the road in traffic's direction and keep to the side.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts and ends near the Girgenti Bus Stop in the outskirts of Siggiewi, served by bus route 62 from Valletta. Vehicle parking in the area is limited - at the side of Triq ir-Remel can be best option. Coordinates to start point: 35.856554, 14.421567.

highlights.title: The route
highlights.description: This trail is a walk through Malta’s highest countryside, linking the famous hilltop Laferla Cross with the lush, green Girgenti Valley. You will experience a mix of steep limestone paths with panoramic views and quiet, shaded lanes filled with fruit trees and historic chapels. It’s a route that perfectly captures the island's unique blend of rugged nature and deep-rooted religious traditions.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: You will trek along well-surfaced paths, parts of which are cobbled, and taking on moderate elevation gain along the Laferla Cross path. Trekking shoes are recommended but trek is doable in trainers, especially in dry weather conditions.

highlights.title: Safety Warnings
highlights.description: The route concludes along a short stretch of busy and fast vehicular road. Cross safely to the left side of the road in traffic's direction and keep to the side for safety. If you wish to avoid this altogether, head straight instead of downhill when reaching the open-air chapel. This offers a safer route back.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: The route does not cross through any villages or amenities, so make sure to bring water, snacks and whatever required from the start.

treks.name: Wied Qirda & Hesri
treks.shortDescription: Two connecting lush valleys between Siggiewi and Zebbug

checkpoints.checkpointText: From this road you can already get an amazing wide view of the valley and the parallel paths we will be taking. First we will take the path on the right to take the south side of Wied Hesri and reach Wied Qirda in Zebbug (after taking an underpass).

Rebuilt in 1675, this chapel houses the tomb of its founder, Fr Bartolomeo Magro, who was tragically murdered during a robbery at his nearby farmhouse in 1722.  Look closely at the chapel’s exterior limestone walls to find dozens of 17th-century maritime graffiti—intricate carvings of galleons and ships etched by sailors as offerings for surviving storms at sea. This secluded sanctuary was a favorite of Malta's national poet, Dun Karm Psaila, who dedicated one of his famous sonnets to its profound silence.

Literally meaning the Valley of Destruction the valley's name likely stems from either the violent flash floods that surge through its bed after heavy rains or the historical memory of 15th-century pirate raids. This Special Area of Conservation is also a vital sanctuary for rare flora, including Malta’s national tree, the Sandarac Gum Tree, and the elusive wild tulip.

checkpoints.id: Resting Spot & Path to Wied il-Kbir
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: Resting Spot & Path to Wied il-Kbir
checkpoints.checkpointText: The small opening offers a good spot where to take a rest with some open views of the valley. From this open spot, there is a rugged green path that takes to Wied il-Kbir (discovered in another trek) and takes to the Tomb of the English Lady carved into the rocks.

As we reach the end of the surfaced road, enjoy this long-sighted view of how Wied il-Kbir valley meanders within its cliff edges. We explore this large valley and its hidden gems in one of our other treks.

Along this 2km long valley you could notice the traditional stone dams designed to slow rushing rainwater, allowing local farmers to irrigate nearby fields even in drier months. Unlike many other valleys, Wied Ħesri remains remarkably untouched, serving as a vital sanctuary for native almond trees that often blossom as early as February. The temporary pools that form after heavy rains  often teem with tadpoles and provide a quiet drinking spot for local bird species.

The chapel’s position on the edge of the Wied Ħesri plateau was strategic; its bell served as a clock for the local farmers, signaling the start and end of their long working days in the surrounding valleys. The cemetery contains the grave of just one British casualty from the Second World War, Gunner V. A. R. Chappell, making it one of the smallest official Commonwealth sites in Malta.
We conclude this trek at this small playground where you can take a deserved rest with a panoramic view over the valley of Wied Hesri. Hope you enjoyed it!

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Trail starting and end points are near the Siggiewi Football Ground, with the parallel road offering plenty of parking spaces. Specifically the route starts near the 'Armonija' bus stop, severd by route 62 from Valletta. Coordinates to start point: 35.857921, 14.436082.

highlights.title: The route
highlights.description: This trek traverses the main paths across two valleys adjoining Siggiewi and Zebbug - Wied Hesri and Wied Qirda. Both will be taken in either direction - offering different perspectives of these beautiful, green and quiet valley paths.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Route goes for the major part along easy and well-surfaced paths - with only a short stretch of soily but easy path at the end of Wied Hesri. Trek can be easily done in trainers in dry conditions. Little elevation change as we remain mostly along valley bed paths.

highlights.title: Safety Warnings
highlights.description: For a very short stretch at the end of the trek, route passess through a busy road. Keep safely on the side.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: The residential area close to the starting point has a number of small groceries/ markets, as well as a playground with public toilet at nearby Mattia Preti Street. A snack bar with WC also available at football club, if open at the time.

treks.name: Wied Hazrun - Ghajn Tejtes
treks.shortDescription: From Dingli Cliffs to panoramic views of Ghajn Tejtes

checkpoints.checkpointText: We are on Panoramic Road offering some of the most iconic views from atop Dingli Cliffs. This Northern stretch of the road is less frequented but not less spectacular. Also look out for a small path towards the cliffs that leads to a viewpoint balcony for unobstructed views.

checkpoints.id: Small Paved Square with St. Paul Statue
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: Small Paved Square with St. Paul Statue
checkpoints.checkpointText: A particular spot consisting of a small paved square with a structure having a statue of St. Paul.
At this building having a niche with a statue of Madonna and child, take the road on its left side to head to views of Wied Hazrun.

Open view over Wied Hazrun, which is one of Dingli’s most ecologically rich valleys that sustains some of Malta’s oldest cultivated fields and rare native holm oaks. Characterised by its terraced slopes, garigue, and maquis habitats. One has also views onto  Ghajn Tejtes valley where we will be heading next.

The name Għajn Tejtes preserves the memory of a natural spring, recalling the importance of freshwater sources in sustaining Malta’s rural landscapes and communities. Surrounded by open countryside, it remains a place where the island’s geology, water heritage, and traditional pathways are closely intertwined.

Now have arrived at the Northest end of Dingli Panoramic road. From this vantage point, the landscape opens out toward Ras id-Dawwara, a rugged headland defined by sheer cliffs and the meeting of land and sea. The view highlights the stark beauty of Malta’s western coastline, where wind, rock, and water have shaped a dramatic and ever-changing natural scene. Ras id-Dawwara is a key highlight and landmark in one of our other treks starting from Migra il-Ferha.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: The route starts from the well known Dingli Panoramic Road, near the small roundabout between the Northern and Southern parts of the road. Parking may be searched along this road or perhaps better just before the mentioned roundabout. Bus route 52 directly connects Dingli to Valletta. Starting GPS coordinates: 35.855742, 14.377155.

highlights.title: The route
highlights.description: First heads in the Mtahleb direction to reach panoramic views of Wied Hazrun and Ghajn Tejtes. Then heads back to the Northern end of the Panoramic Road before returning to the starting point with views over the cliffs along the way back.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Route is all on tarmaced or cemented roads, so can be done in comfortable trainers or walking shoes.

highlights.title: Safety Warnings
highlights.description: A bit of a longer route is taken to circumvent private property. Whilst all roads were public at the time of route design, always be respectful of private property and check for any changes in access rights or conditions before embarking on the trek.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: The Cliffs restaurant and visitor centre near the start/end points is the only main strucutre along the cliffs and route - it is the most reliable option for restoration or WC facilities.

treks.name: Lunzjata - Wied Liemu
treks.shortDescription: One of the lushest treks in Malta with unique green spots and paths

checkpoints.checkpointText: As you start getting glimpses of the green areas we will be exploring, half way though the upcoming downhill take this path on the left - one of the most scenic and characteristic paths in the Maltese Islands.

Built in the 16th century, this chapel is dedicated to the Annunciation and is one of the older rural churches in the area, reflecting early post-medieval religious practice. Nearby, the Dar tal-Irtiri was later established as a retreat house, used for spiritual exercises and quiet reflection within a secluded setting.

We make a U-turn around this charming Maltese farmhouse and head along Santa Keterina Road for a peaceful walk and some more discoveries. Observe this particular insigna following the turn, just right after which you also come in front of Tax-Xierfa Museum - a museum dedicated to preserving the rich agricultural heritage of Malta.

Have now reached the tiny hamlet of Santa Katerina, known as Tad-Daħla, meaning entry, probably because this area was once a main entry point to Rabat. The few houses here are built around the small church of Saint Catherine, a church with a long history spanning over 500 years. It was first built here in the year 1500 and was one of the 30 countryside chapels which fell under the auspice of the old capital of Malta, Mdina.

An amazing vantage point, revealing terraced fields shaped over centuries to make use of the natural contours. One of greenest and most fertile landscape sustained by seasonal water flow creating an amazing green corridor. A view that remains impressed in memory for its beauty and uniqueness in the Maltese Islands.

We will be later heading back to the starting point via the countryside path on the right, but before that, head straight for a while and then take a path on the left for another unique and spectacular view.

Another amazing and quite unique spot, completely surrounding by greenery. The view of the trees in the middle of the plain is quite a sight. Behind them lies the village of Dingli, specifically the agricultural estate known as Dar il-Bniet, literally meaning House of Daughters. Local folklore suggests it refers to former female inhabitants, with legends of three mysterious girls sometimes seen crossing the road on moonlit nights. Take in the surroundings before heading back along the path referred to earlier.

Hope you are enjoying this incredibly peaceful path with agricultural fields on one side and the valley cliffs we walked atop earlier on the other side. Notice the small canal and bridges which cross over waterways following some heavy rainfall - a water system from the Knights era!

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: The route starts and ends at the outskirts of Rabat and Mtarfa, near the Ghajn National Water Conservation Centre. There is a small parking lot just next to the Centre. Direct bus routes 52 and 56 can be taken from Valletta. Starting GPS coordinates: 35.884221, 14.390732.

highlights.title: The route
highlights.description: Starts from the Rabat rural area known as Ghar Barka and heads to some amazing pathways the lush agricultural lands and valleys of the tal-Lunzjata and Santa Katerina areas, behore making the way back across the lush Wied Liemu. The route offers some of the greenest and nature immersed views on the islands in Winter - we will discover some of the most scenic and spots.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Route is almost entierly on asphalted or concreted paths within countryside fields and agricultural lands, with moderate elevation changes. Only a short stretch for the discovery of a hidden gem of a view is on a dirt track which can be very muddy following rainfall. So doable with trainers but trekking boots are more recommnended since this is a strong water catchement area.

highlights.title: Safety Warnings
highlights.description: Always respect private property - some parts of the route are at risk of becoming private property (even if they were not when this route was reconned).

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: WCs facilities near the Ghajn National Water Conservation Centre - a food truck also often stations close to this centre. Otherwise, the route is along quiet residential and rural areas with no particular amenities - so do bring enough water and snacks for the trek.

treks.name: Hal Safi Loop
treks.shortDescription: Hal Safi Loop - a quiet walk within a traditional and historic Maltese village and its countryside surroundings

checkpoints.checkpointText: An 18th century chapel dedicated to St. Mary just a few steps away from the village's main parish church dedicated to the Conversion of St. Paul. First mention of a chapel on this site dates back to 1575 giving it historical significance as the oldest standing church in the locality. Historically, the chapel was known for its hospitality; during the feast of the Assumption (15th August), it traditionally opened its doors to provide a meal for the village's needy.

This is a historic, public space named after Captain Sir Alexander Ball, the first British Civil Commissioner of Malta. Originally built with permission from King George III, this garden is one of a series of such gardens commissioned between 1802 and 1808, and was planted with trees, vines, and herbs to promote local self-sufficiency. Captain Ball, who admired these gardens, frequently visited, with the Ħal Safi garden being one of his favourites. Its main feature today is the central pathway with trellis and various trees and bushes exhibited on either side. At the end of the pathway there is a designated beekeeping area where hives are maintained.

Named after the medieval noble Ħal Antun, this square preserves one of the oldest place names in the area, reflecting feudal land ownership in late medieval Malta. It developed as a small village core, historically serving as a meeting point for surrounding agricultural communities and local routes. Today it is a quiet open spaces with some benches where you can take a mid-trek rest.

A meticulously restored 18th-century windmill built by the Knights of Malta, notable for being the only operational windmill on the islands that still retains its original mechanisms. Directly adjacent to the mill is a significant complex of Paleo-Christian catacombs and tombs dating back to the Roman period, which feature unique rock-cut window tombs and decorative pilasters.

Uniquely situated on top of a section of the Tax-Xarolla catacombs lies St. Andrew's Chapel. Rebuilt in 1634, the simple rectangular structure features a square-headed door and a central bell-cot, though it was later used as a farm building before a dedicated restoration by Din l-Art Ħelwa.

A a 2,000-year-old masonry remnant likely belonging to a rural Roman farm or villa. This scheduled Class B national monument is built from large ashlar blocks and features specialized Lewis bolt slots once used for hoisting the stones into place. It is one of the few surviving examples of classical Roman architecture still visible in a rural Maltese setting.

The Safi Parish Church, dedicated to the Conversion of St. Paul, is a classic Baroque structure built between 1712 and 1744, featuring a striking façade with two bell towers and an interior housing a titular painting by Stefano Erardi. The church overlooks the village’s main Square, a peaceful, traditional space that serves as the heart of the community and is surrounded by typical Maltese townhouses and narrow winding streets.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts and ends at the Safi Parish church and square - parking spaces within the square are limited with better chances in the village outskirts near the Safi playground (Xarolla area). Bus route 73 serves Safi from Valletta. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.833390, 14.483817.

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: Travels from the centre of one Malta's smallest and most traditional villages known for its quiet, time-stands-still atmosphere, characterized by narrow winding streets and a historic village core. The route then takes to village's very quite and peaceful countryside roads along which some interesting landmarks are to be discovered.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Trek is entirely on asphalted surfaces and can be done easily in trainers to keep light footed and comfortable.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Safi village core has some small grocery shops and bars. WC facilities near the Safi playground and Local Council at Triq il-Menqa, not too distant from the starting and ending points (and a good place to look for parking).

treks.name: Wied Ghomor & Mensija
treks.shortDescription: Green lung of the East

checkpoints.checkpointText: First glimples of the Wied Ghomor greener as we walk under a flyover road.
Take the road on the left for a path into the valley

You have reached the heart of the vallery and its greener vegetation. Enjoy the peaceful surroundings. You can venture further if feeling adventorous and conditions allow but this routes goes back along the same path we came from.
From the short flight of steps enjoy an elevated view of the Mensija Valley we'll be heading to next
Again surronded by vegetation, we are in the heart of the second valley of the trek - Wied Mensija.

A suggestive picnic area with views over the valley. A good spot for a snack break before we head back to the coast. We will next walk for a short while on a busy road. Take care, especially crossing the road
Ghomor Valley on one Side and Spinola Bay on the other, with the St. Julian's Parish church right in front. One of the more modern churches built in 1961 and visited by the Pope in 1989.
One of the most picturesque and iconic bays in Malta, with vibrant colours from the colourful luzzus and lively atmosphere. Picturesque in different ways during both the day and night

The small sandy beach overlooked by splendid Neo-Gothic facade of the Church of Our Lady of Mount Carmel, make this another iconic hotspot in the Maltese islands. The interior Gothic architecture of the church is equally impressive. This promenade stretch offers a magnificient relaxing spot to end the trek.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: The route starts from the well known Spinola Bay, near the `LOVE` monument. Bus routes 13, 14 and 16 run from Valletta and Sliema to the bay. Routes 202, 212 and 222 arrive from Northern regions. Finding parking can be challenging - the Ta' Giorni area in nearby San Gwann is a quieter option and not too distant. Route ends in nearby Balluta Bay. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.9191, 14.4900.

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: A scenic walk starting from coast and heading into the green lung of the area - Wied Ghomor and Mensija. Returns to the coastal promenade, ending at the picturesque Balluta bay.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: For the major part on asphalted roads, venturing into some valley paths. Can be done in trainers in dry conditions. Little elevation change.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: A small part of the route is along busy roads and contains a couple of busy road crossings. Take caution and use zebra crossing/ lights where possible

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: The starting and ending locations are at very touristic areas with a wide variety of tourist amenities. Half way through, the route passes by a quiet picnic area with tables - so bringing a packed lunch is also a good idea!

treks.name: Gharghur Victoria Lines Trail
treks.shortDescription: Victory Lines Trail and Valleys of Gharghur

checkpoints.checkpointText: A lovely and skilfully crafted minitiature statue of the Parish church of St. Bartholomew, that had characterised the trek starting point. Route now takes a scenic path overlooking Wied Anglu and reaching the area known as Birguma, before we actually had down into the valley along the Victoria Lines

Having reached the Birguma residential area with its villas, now look for a rightside path for the path along the Victoria Lines. Part of a 19th-century British defensive line stretching across the island, this path follows fortifications built to create a strategic land barrier. We will take the Gharghur part of the path with old walls, watch posts, and natural escarpments that once formed a continuous line of defense. Worth taking some safe lookbacks along the way for more scenic views. Take care.
Hope you enjoy the highly scenic experience of descending and ascending the Wied Anglu valleybed along the historic Victoria Lines. Here is an iconic landmark badge as a memory of the experience.

These rock-cut cave are believed to have been used since antiquity, taking advantage of the soft limestone to create simple yet effective shelters. Archaeological findings suggest periods of habitation and reuse, offering insight into early rural life and adaptation to the landscape. What a home view they used to have!

Hope you enjoyed the amazing wide vistas from the different viewpoints from this area known as 'Top of the Word'. Here is a main viewing point where you can also take a break with a view on the benches that overlook the Malta's North East area and on a clear day as far as Gozo and Comino. Right ahead you are looking at the Bahar ic-Caghaq coastal area. After a small break, the route continues along the Victoria Lines heading down another valley.

Again another snenic valley descent and ascent along the Victoria Lines, this time along Wied Faham. You are actually now standing within the San Giovanni Battery - a late 19th-century British military fortification completed in December 1898 it was designed to house quick-firing guns and machine guns, and featuring concrete platforms and an underground magazine.

If fatigue has not taken over, you can now take a small and quiet circular route around Fort Madliena and its ditch, and amongst surrounding trees. Constructed by the British in the late 19th century, Fort Madliena formed part of the Victoria Lines defensive system designed to divide and protect the island. Its polygonal layout, deep ditch, and caponier defenses reflect advances in military engineering of the period.
A highly picturesque and photographed spot, this is a charming stone bridge spanning the Wied id-Dis valley. Enjoy the views over the valley

A beuatiful chapel perched on a flight of steps facing scenic valley views, this is a locally cherished chapel formerly known as Santa Marija taz-Zellieqa chapel. Dating back to 1530 and rebult in 1650.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts from the Gharghur Parish church dedicated to St. Bartholomew and ends at a nearby valley area behind the church. Since finding parking near the churc is difficult, looking for parking in the residential area behind it provides better options. Bus routes 46 and 260 connect Valletta and Gharghur. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.923972, 14.453204.

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: This route explores varuious valleys surrounding Gharghur (Wied Anglu, Wied Faham and Wied id-Dis), with a main section of it being along the Victoria Lines trail section for spanning from the Birguma area to Madliena.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Sections on the Victoria Lines path are quite rugged and involve descending and climbing a substantial number of high stepped stairs, and a significant elevation change within short distance. Trekking shoes are recommended for safety and comfort.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Take care along the Victoria Lines path as some sections edge over the valleys with no side walls. This route should be done in dry and non-windy conditions for safety, and not recommended for those with fear of heights.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Local band clubs and bars are located directly near the church square at the starting point, where you can enjoy a snack or a drink in these tranditional bars and which typically have restroom facilities available for patrons. The church itself has restroom facilities. Benches where one can rest with a view are avaialable at the Top of the World area, right in between the two main efforts of this trek.

treks.name: Wied il-Kbir & Wied Qirda
treks.shortDescription: The larget valley system in Malta

checkpoints.checkpointText: We are coming across the second small bridge over the valley bed. You can take the short path on the left amongst the trees in one direction and stick to the road when coming back the other direction.

You will come across some water management related structures as Wied il-Kbir houses one of Malta's most significant historical and modern water management systems. Originally developed in the late 19th century, it continues to be a critical node in the national network managed by the Water Services Corporation. The system was largely the brainchild of British engineer Sir Osbert Chadwick in the 1880s.
We will be using all the paths in this crossroads - take first the leftest one for a hidden gem discovery and then when coming back take the one straight ahead

ALso known as Ghar Tas-Slaleb because of the carved crosses at the start of the carve. Hence it is believed to have been used as a medieval church. Venture into only where you know you can safely return and with appropriate ligthing.
A nice small open area where you can sit on a rock to take in the surroundings and maybe a snack too before proceeding with more challenging parts of the trek

We will now take a scenic and more rugged path within the valley. So we will not have checkpoints for a while. There is mainly a single path along the route, with a single main cross path. Always stick to the one mostly to your right.
This elevated spot offers one of the most iconic viewpoints over the valley. Take some time to enjoy it and take it all in.

checkpoints.id: More Views on onto fields & valley
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: More Views on onto fields & valley
checkpoints.checkpointText: The quarry on left side is no great sight but on the otherside you can look over for some more amazing views over the fields and valleys. Also see if you can spot this traditional Maltese well.
Enjoy the amazing view of the steep valley along which we started the route and reached Ghar Hanzir. The earned badge will also keep this memory for you!
We will now trek a part of the Wied Qirda valley bed, which is narrower and its rock faces offer interesting formations. For instance look out for the following resemebling an elephant's head!

checkpoints.id: The Tomb of the English Lady
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: The Tomb of the English Lady
checkpoints.checkpointText: A mysterious rock-carved doorway believed by some to be a tomb, alhough no remains have been found within. thought to have been built by Major Patrick Yule of the Royal Engineers around 1842-43, possibly in memory of a Lycian expedition. Locals also associate it with the burial of an English woman who died in the area. You can proceed with trekking along this path through thicker vegetation and which takes you to the main upper path of Wied Qirda (explored in another route). This routes concludes by trekking back to our starting point.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Starting point most easily reached by private vehicle or ride share, with possibility of side road parking at the start of the valley. Otherwise, bus routes 73 and 74 from Valletta have 10-minute away stops. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.87060, 14.47625.

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: The route is in the form of a semi-loop, starting and ending at the start of Wied il-Kbir Valley from Qormi side. Trekking along a large valley system we will explore Wied il-Kbir and Wied Qirda and explore some of their interesting geological formations and vegetation.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Large parts of the trek route goes along rugged and rockey paths, so trekking boots are necessary. This is also a main water catchemnt area that can hold water and muddy paths for several days. Elevation change is moderate.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Cave discovered along the way - venture only where you are sure you can safely return and with appropriate ligthing. Rocky paths can be slippery, even with trekking boots.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: This is a trek fully immersed in nature with no amenities or facilities along the way. So bring plenty of water and food for re-fuelling at some of the amazing open areas and viewpoints of this trek.

treks.name: Wied is-Sewda
treks.shortDescription: Valley connecting Qormi-Attard-Zebbug

checkpoints.checkpointText: This small chapel is dedicated to Our Lady and reflects the long tradition of Marian devotion found in the Maltese countryside. Its name “tal-Blat” (“of the rock”) likely refers to the rocky ground or stone outcrop on which the chapel was built. It's lighting at night adds charm to the chapel.
You are now on a long path leading to Attard and overlooking the main Wied is-Sewda path. Enjoy the peaceful surroundings along various farmhouses and niches with statues dedicated to St. Mary

You will soon reach the end of this path after which we need to take a left for a very short stretch along a main road before taking again a left to enter the main Wied is-Sewda path whose entrance is displayed in the image.

Wied is-Sewda is one of the longest valleys on the island, cutting through the landscape as it carries seasonal rainwater toward the coast. Its name, meaning the black valley, may derive from the dark soil and shaded rock formations found along parts of its course.
Route now takes a detour to the uphill countryside roads that will lead us to the Tal-Hlas Chapel with some scenic view spots over the valley along the way

This chapel is dedicated to Our Lady of Deliverance (tal-Ħlas), reflecting a long-standing devotion to the Virgin Mary as a protector and intercessor in times of need. Its traditional stone architecture and rural setting echo the centuries-old practice of building small chapels as places for prayer and thanksgiving in the Maltese countryside.

This parish church, dedicated to St. George, was completed in the late 17th century and is one of Qormi's two historic parish churches. Designed in the Baroque style, it reflects the town’s growth during that period

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: The trek starts towards the end of Qormi Valley Road and ends at the nearby San Gorg Parish church. Finding parking along Valley Road can be challenging, unless proceeding to its end. Bus routes 61 and 63 from Valletta get you close to the starting point. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.88308, 14.46882

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: The route is designed over paths in Wied is-Sewda that lies between the towns of Qormi, Attard and Zebbug. It fast takes a countryside path from Qormi to Attard overlooking the main Wied is-Sewda path which will be then taken in the reverse direction from Attard. From the main path we divert uphill to the tal-Hlas Chapel before heading to Qormi's historic town roads to conclude route at St. George Parish Church.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Although almost entirely on countryside paths, the route is fully asphalted roads so can be done in trainers. If on or following rainy days, trekking shoes are more adbvisable.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Some cafes and bars can be found near St. George Parish Church marking the end of the route. Qormi is renowned for its local bakeries and sweets shops - good places for a treat after the trek!

treks.name: Wied Qannotta - Ghajn Rihana
treks.shortDescription: For amazing green views with a touch of historic heritage

checkpoints.checkpointText: The trek starts from the Burmarrad Parish Church of the Immaculate Heart of Mary - a modern Roman Catholic sanctuary established in 1971 and managed by the Franciscan Conventual Friars.

archaeological site features the most extensive remains of a Roman agricultural villa ever discovered in Malta, including ancient olive-pressing equipment and fortifications. Managed by Heritage Malta, the site is traditionally believed to be where St. Publius welcomed St. Paul after his shipwreck, though it remains closed to the public except for special open days.

We now take the path to Wied Qannotta -  a scenic, fertile valley stretching between Burmarrad, Wardija and Bidnija. As a typical Maltese wadi, it serves as a natural watercourse during the rainy season, supporting local agriculture and vital groundwater collection.

The short uphill path has taken us this derelict 16th-century wayside chapel. Originally dedicated to the Nativity of Our Lady, it was rededicated to the Immaculate Conception in the 18th century following a rebuilding. Make sure to also take in the amazing views over the valley from this spot.
Take a moment to enjoy one of the greenest viewpoints in Malta, overlooking the fertile valley of Wied Qannotta nestled between the Wardija and Bidnija hills

The downhill path with the green views onlooking the Targa Gap ridge, has taken us to the chapel of St. Margaret. Though its exact origins are unknown, it was first documented in 1575 by Monsignor Pietro Dusina and later rebuilt in its current form in the 18th century.

We have now reached a recently regenerated path alongside the natural watercourse of Ghajn Rihana. The name likely refers to the myrtle plant (riħan), and the valley features a stream that flows for most of the year, particularly after the winter rains.

Hidden amongst the farmhouses lies the Tal Qadi Temple - a megalithic temple site dating back to the Tarxien phase (c. 3300–3000 BC). A famous globigerina limestone slab was discovered here in 1927, featuring incised star-like symbols and a crescent moon. Many scholars believe it represents one of the world's oldest lunar calendars or star maps.

We have now reached the most renowned Bird Park in Malta, as well as St. Michael Chapel. This is a late 16th-century wayside chapel, rebuilt in its present form in the 18th century. Characterised by simple facade and small bell-cot, standing as a historic landmark within the fertile Għajn Riħana valley.
Thanks for experiencing with us this trek, which now approaches the end through the quiet fields of the Burmarrad plain before reaching again the Burmarrad Parish Church.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Start and end from near Burmarrad Parish church. From Valletta, buses 41, 42, 45, 48, or 250 offer a direct route to the area. Vehicle parking best sought in side streets or proceeding further near Lidl supermarket.  GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.933550, 14.415173.

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: From the hamlet of Burmarrad, the route heads into the scenic valley of Wied Qannotta, before looping back through the Ghajn Rihana valley whilst exploring scenic views and some hidden historic landmarks.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Within Wied Qannotta, the path is a mix of soily and rocky terrain, with some short uphill sections. Certain areas can also hold muddy waters. Trekking shoes are hence recommended. The rest of the route is largely on asphalted or level paths.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: One major road crossings but with pedestrian traffic lights available - use them for safety.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Food outlets and small bars/ pubs present in the centre of Burmarrad hamlet near the start and end points of the route. The rest of the route is immeersed in nature.

treks.name: Fiddien - Bingemma
treks.shortDescription: From Chadwick Lakes to Bingemma

checkpoints.checkpointText: Recently opened trail along Fiddien Valley to Chadwick Lakes. The area got its name after the intervention of Sir Osbert Chadwick, when during the second half of the 19th century planned several dams, of various sizes and forms, with the intention to retain as much rainwater as possible.

You have now reached the main water catchment area of the Qlejgha Valley, forming the most searched scene  within the Chadwick lakes system. The stream then continues to meander around the valley floor, forming small pools, or even small waterfalls, wherever there are depressions in the ground. The amount of water present in the valley is very seasonal.

The uphill path on the left side takes us to the planned route to Bingemma but it is suggested to first continue for the rest of the road ahead to enjoy in full the Qlejgha valley, and then return to this mark.

The climb was worth it. Has taken us to this hlll known as il-Qolla with 360 degree scenic views. Venture into the rock formations of the hill for more amazing views. A great place to take a snack break in some shade provided by the trees.

Reaching near the Bingemma cliff edge, you can enjoy views over the Bingemma Gap with the Victoria Lines on the left, Our Lady of Itria chapel ahead, the town of Mgarr on the right and the Gozo channel ahead.

We have already been walking along the Victoria Lines - a 12km British line of fortifications along the width of Malta - but we have now reached one of its most scenic and photographed spots within the Bingemma Gap

checkpoints.id: Chapel of Our Lady of Itria
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: Chapel of Our Lady of Itria
checkpoints.checkpointText: A humble rural chapel quietly overlooking the surrounding valleys. Built centuries ago by local farmers and travelers, it has long served as a peaceful waypoint for those crossing these Maltese hills.
A small countryside chapel that has watched over this path for generations. Pilgrims and villagers once stopped here to rest and pray, making it a quiet landmark along Rabat’s old rural routes.

As we reach the end of the route, we go over this bridge over Wied il-Fiddien which we had gone under in the first part of the Chadwick Lakes trail. Take a moment to again enjoy the serenity of the Fiddien Valley.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts and ends near entrance to Chadwick Lakes trail. By bus one can take routes 50, 51, 52, 53 from Valletta to Rabat (Stop at Saqqajja) and then change route to 109, and stop at Fiddien. By car there are limited parking spaces available near entrance - for less parking worries, leave vehicle near Għajn National Water Conservation Centre and take a 500m walk to start route. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.886291, 14.380207

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: First takes the recently regenerated and incredibly scenic path to Chadwick Lakes and walk within the Fidden and Qlejgha valleys, before heading up towards the Bingemma cliff viewpoints and Victoria lines at Bingemma Gap. A shorter return route is taken along vehicle roads surrunded by greenery.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Crossing Fiddien and Qlejgha valley paths, as well as rocky terrain at Bingemma Gap. Possible with trainer shoes in dry conditions but trekking shoes recommended.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Keep safe and do not take risks near Bingemma Cliff Edges.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: No restrooms or food outlets along the route, so think ahead and bring water and snacks with you. On Weekends, there are usually some stands with traditional Maltese snacks set up near Chadwick Lakes trail entrance.

treks.name: Ahrax - Ghadira Side
treks.shortDescription: The Sourthern Coast of Ahrax with views onto Ghadira Bay and discovering hidden bays.

checkpoints.checkpointText: Immediately take this path on the left to leave the vehicle road and proceed on a greener path that leads to the cliff side and some hidden bay gems.

This circular cove is a prime example of a collapsed doline, where the roof of an underground sea cave gave way to the elements over thousands of years. Look closely at the cliff faces; the layered limestone reveals the ancient seabed’s history, often hiding fossilized remains of sea urchins and shells.
A 'beach' characterised by a platform, often used by shore-entry divers who prefer its direct access to the deeper reef walls.

`One of the few constructed and surviving entrenchment walls built by the Order of St. John in 1761. This was intended as part of a network of sea walls designed to present a physical barrier to invasion. In practice, this strategy involved the construction of many miles of fortifications, but the ambitious plans ran into financial difficulties and was not completed. This entrenchment is one of the few completed and well preserved examples.

We had to get back to the road to respect private propery but now we can return to the off beaten track. As the sign warns, the path in this part will go very close to cliff edge. If you wish to avoid that, you can proceed a bit further on the road and then join the same path later.

You have just crossed one of the most popular and scenic camping spots in Malta, should you wish to return for a different camping adventure. Now follow the app's route market to find this path that takes to Slugs Bay.

Locals often call it Dħalet ix-Xilep, named after the Xilpa (Dreamfish or Salema porgy) commonly found in these waters. The beach was formed by a rare geological accident where massive boulders tumbled from the cliffs to create a sheltered basin that naturally traps fine sand. It earned its English nickname from the dark sea cucumbers often found on the seabed, which early British military personnel mistakenly identified as slugs.

Perched on the cliff edge, this small chapel was originally built to serve the local farming and fishing community who lived far from the village center. Legend says it was built on this specific spot to offer a final blessing to sailors departing for the dangerous crossing toward Sicily. This is the final landmark for the route, you may return back from the same route for a different perspective or take the long straight road back for a quicker and easier return.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route start and ends close to the 'Msid' bus stop at the start of Ahrax road. This is served by buses 41 and 42 from Valletta and bus 222 from Sliema area. If looking for parking, the spaces in the uphill road beyond Ghadira bay is the closest option. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.973777, 14.348792

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: First takes an amazing hidden path to reach the southern cliffs of the Ahrax peninsula overlooking Ghadira Bay, to then discover some hidden bays. Once reaching the end of the peninsula, one could take the same route back for a different perspective or take the long straight road back for a quicker and easier return.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Mostly on the off beaten track with some rocky parts, so trekking shoes are highly recommended. The return can be made along a long but quiet straight vehicular road, still with pleasant views.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: As sign posted on the road, a part of this trek goes along the very edge of the cliff subject to coastal erosion. One could proceed along the vehicular road in this path and rejoin the marked route at a later stage. Take caution in the more challenging descents of this trek and keep safe distance along cliff edges. Respect private property which may have changed from the recon of this trek.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Public restrooms and some snack kiosks/ restaurants available at the Ghadira Bay located downhill from the starting/ end point, but otherwise the route is fully immersed in nature with no amenities.

treks.name: Selmun - Mgiebah
treks.shortDescription: From Selmun through Mgiebah Bay and to Tunnaras in Mellieha

checkpoints.checkpointText: Selmun Chapel.
Imgiebah Bay is a scenic spot offering stunning views of the surrounding landscape.
Selmun Palace is a historic site with significant cultural value.
Irdum Ghajn Hadid is a hidden gem offering a peaceful retreat in the heart of the countryside.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts and ends near entrance to Chadwick Lakes trail. By bus one can take routes 50, 51, 52, 53 from Valletta to Rabat (Stop at Saqqajja) and then change route to 109, and stop at Fiddien. By car there are limited parking spaces available near entrance - for less parking worries, leave vehicle near Għajn National Water Conservation Centre and take a 500m walk to start route. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.956695, 14.379940

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: First takes the recently regenerated and incredibly scenic path to Chadwick Lakes and walk within the Fidden and Qlejgha valleys, before heading up towards the Bingemma cliff viewpoints and Victoria lines at Bingemma Gap. A shorter return route is taken along vehicle roads surrunded by greenery.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Crossing Fiddien and Qlejgha valley paths, as well as rocky terrain at Bingemma Gap. Possible with trainer shoes in dry conditions but trekking shoes recommended.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Keep safe and do not take risks near Bingemma Cliff Edges.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: No restrooms or food outlets along the route, so think ahead and bring water and snacks with you. On Weekends, there are usually some stands with traditional Maltese snacks set up near Chadwick Lakes trail entrance.

treks.name: Qammiegh - Latnija
treks.shortDescription: Amazing changes in sceneries and attractions in a single trek

checkpoints.checkpointText: On your left side, you are now overlooking the Ghadira Nature Reserve. This seven-hectare wetland is a vital Mediterranean stopover for over 200 migratory bird species, including flamingos and herons. It preserves a rare mosaic of salt marshes and sand dunes, providing a protected habitat for indigenous flora and breeding residents like the Little Ringed Plover. Visitors can observe these seasonal populations from concealed hides overlooking brackish lagoons and tamarisk groves.
Take a look back and we hope that the offered view onto Ghadira Bay, and also onto the Majjistral Nature Park (coastal side) made the steep climb worth it.
Now take this path that will take us down to the coastal edge. Quite steep so take it slow and steady but we will pass through an amazing spot along the way.
This unique view of the path passing through fallen off boulders is worth an hidden gem badge for long-lasting memories. Route takes the path on the left that continues the descent.
Now taking a short coastal stretch offering a different kind of scenery. You can follow the rocks with orange/ red marks to guide you in this part of the route.
An amazing spot with large boulders fallen off from the Qammiegh cliffs and impermeable Blue Clay slopes creating a tiered andscape of gullies and ridges. Take the path right in between the slopes.

This natural rock arch was formed by the collapse of a large sea cave along the rugged northern cliffs, creating a scenic frame for the Mediterranean. Tucked away near hidden boat houses, it remains one of the archipelago's most northern and least-frequented geological features.

This massive, collapsed limestone cavern features a vertical drop that has allowed a sheltered micro-forest of indigenous Carob and Bay Laurel trees to flourish within its walls. Groundbreaking excavations at this site recently rewrote national history, revealing that human habitation in Malta began 8,500 years ago—a full millennium earlier than previously believed.  Because of its newfound status as a heritage at risk and the presence of sensitive archaeological deposits still in situ, the cave is currently closed to the public and protected by an emergency conservation order.

This stone arch serves as a historic gateway, framing an elevated panoramic view of the Gozo Channel and the distant islands of Comino and Gozo. Beyond the archway, a series of rock-cut steps—some potentially dating back to Roman times—descend the rugged ridge toward the shoreline.

This secluded, north-facing inlet is famed for its pale sands and exceptionally clear, turquoise waters that reveal vast underwater seagrass meadows from the cliffs above. The bay is naturally sheltered by high limestone ridges, creating a calm basin that feels worlds away from the busier beaches nearby.
Now taking a long and smoothly surfaced strech of road for a deserved easier stretch but still offering great side views.

Built in 1649 and named as St. Agatha's Tower, this Order of St. John fortification served as the primary defensive link between Valletta and Gozo, communicating signals across the islands via fire and smoke. Its distinct iron-oxide red exterior was designed not just for visibility, but as a psychological deterrent to Ottoman invaders approaching from the sea. From its roof, the 360-degree panorama offers a rare view of both the northern and southern coastlines of Malta simultaneously.

Trek concludes with a descent through Foresta 2000 - a sprawling reforestation project has transformed a formerly barren hillside into a flourishing Mediterranean woodland of over 20,000 indigenous trees, including Aleppo Pine, Holm Oak, and Carob. It serves as a vital ecological corridor, linking the Għadira wetlands to the high ridges of Marfa to provide a continuous habitat for protected wildlife like the Mediterranean Chameleon.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route start and end points are at the upper side of Ghadira Bay, near the Nature Reserve and Foresta 2000. Bus 222 takes the most direct route from Valletta to Ghadira. Plenty of parking spaces alongside Ghadira Bay which are quickly taken up in Summer but most of which would be available in Winter when more likely you'll be doing this trek. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.973777, 14.348792

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: One of the most scenic routes amongst all treks which has everything - paths amongst greenery, coastal stretches, clay slopes, natural arches and windows, a cave, bays and a historic tower. Route takes into the area known as Qammiegh, heading to the Qassisu area and a historic cave and tower, before ending the route in a natural Mediterranean woodland.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: You'll be taking rugged and rocky paths, covering coastal stretches, climbing a path within clay slopes and navigating through boulders and stepped paths, so trekking shoes are a necessity. Significant elevation changes on challenging terrain.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: Take caution in the more challenging descents of this trek and keep safe distance along cliff edges. Respect private property which may have changed from the recon of this trek.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Public restrooms and some snack kiosks/ restaurants available at Ghadira Bay near the starting/ end point, but otherwise the route is fully immersed in nature with no amenities.

treks.name: Mellieha Panoramas
treks.shortDescription: A walk within the fantastic village of Mellieha, reaching some of its best open panoramic views

checkpoints.checkpointText: The first main panoramic vantage point of the route, with a fantastic view of Mellieha Bay and the the Ahrax peninsula behind it (covered in other treks) and the left side a scenic view of the Mellieha village and its imposing parish church. Further down, notice a St. Paul statue erected on a hillrock.

During opening hours, you can descend the 70+ steps into this natural cave, transformed into a shrine in 1644 by a Sicilian merchant who rerouted a hidden spring to flow directly at the feet of the Madonna statue.  Similar to the Holy Land’s tradition, this cave was a primary site for expectant mothers or those struggling to nurse, who would visit to drink the miraculous spring water or pray for a safe delivery.

Located just next to Sanctuary of Our Lady, this square commemorates the historic 1990 visit of Pope John Paul II, who knelt here to pray at the ancient cave-shrine of the Madonna. Right beneath your feet is one of the largest underground shelter complexes in Malta (entrance nearby), stretching over 500 meters and including private rooms and a small medical clinic carved directly into the rock. Long before it was a paved square, this plateau was used as a lookout point to spot corsair ships entering Mellieħa Bay - go and take in the view yourself too!

checkpoints.id: Sanctuary of Our Lady of Mellieha
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: Sanctuary of Our Lady of Mellieha
checkpoints.checkpointText: The oldest Marian shrine in Malta, where the centerpiece is a 13th-century fresco of the Madonna and Child painted directly onto the cave wall. Tradition holds that the Evangelist St. Luke himself painted the original image when he was shipwrecked on the island with St. Paul in 60 AD. This sanctuary is so historically significant that it is one of only 20 shrines worldwide to have received a 'Golden Rose' from a Pope, a rare honor signifying its spiritual importance.

checkpoints.id: View onto Mellieha Bay and ta' Qassisu
checkpoints.checkpointTitle: View onto Mellieha Bay and ta' Qassisu
checkpoints.checkpointText: Another vantage point offering a slightly different perspective onto Mellieha Bay and a better panorama of the Ta' Qassisu area and the historic Red Tower, which are explored in of the most amazing treks in our itinerary. The adjacent playground is a great spot for a break with a view, or having a snack with at the cafe with one of the most scenic views on the island.

The benches here offer a great sight onto the Mellieha Cross to which we'll venture to next and the more Western views from Mellieha. If staying in Mellieha, it's a great spot for enjoying a sunset as shown in the picture.

While Gozo is the more famous contender, some scholars argue this rugged cliff-side cave is the 'true' home of the nymph Calypso because its surroundings—fertile valleys and ancient freshwater springs—match Homer’s descriptions in The Odyssey more closely than the red sands of Gozo. These caves were likely inhabited by Byzantine-era settlers who carved the terraces and niches still visible in the rock today.

Perched on a garrigue landscapte, this is often referred to as Is-Salib tal-Pellegrini or the Good Friday Cross. Erected for Good Friday during Covid Times, the cross became a national talking point when a Planning Authority order to remove it was dramatically reversed following a public outcry and intervention by the Prime Minister.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: The walking trail starts and ends near the Mellieha Primary School and Local Council. Parking can be searched in the adjacent side streets, or from Valletta one can take Bus numbers 41 and 42 to the 'Skrajda' bus stop in nearby Mellieha Centre. GPS coordinates to starting point: 35.957359, 14.359973

highlights.title: The Route
highlights.description: From the above starting point, the trail takes a loop around varios spots in Mellieha offering breathtaking panoramic views onto the Northern parts of the island. In between it passess through the historic village centre and some of its interesting locations.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: For the most part is on asphalted roads, except for a short off the beaten path venture to reach the Mellieha Cross. Moderate elevation changes, mainly through the asending and descending of steps. Can be done in trainers, especially in dry conditions.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Half-way through the route, we pass through the village centre which is the main tourist spot with all related amenities and facilities.

treks.shortDescription: From Selmun through Mgiebah Bay and to Tunnaras in Mellieha

treks.name: Gudja-Ghaxaq Countryside
treks.shortDescription: History and Nature

checkpoints.checkpointText: Area known as tax-Xlejli in Gudja. Restroom facilties
Now moving to countryside roads. Although quite quiet, the streets are narrow so keep on the left side
In the distance over the fields you can spot the Ta Loretu chapel. Another landmark you can reach from the centre of Gudja on another trek
Enjoy an iconic view of this trek overlooking the Saptan Valley
One of the three semaphore towers in Malta. We are now in the village of Hal Ghaxaq

checkpoints.checkpointTitle: Tiny Park dedicated to Animal love
checkpoints.checkpointText: A small haven where you can take a break near the monument dedicated to animals and let the little ones name the animals
Enjoy more views over the agricultural fields

The Xlejli Tower also known as Bettina Tower now comes into view. It is thought to have served as a watchtower as far as from Roman or Medieval times but its exact age remains a mystery. It is located within a private Palazzo but you can get closer from the paths on your right

Roman Catholic church dedicated to the Assumption of St. Mary whose feast is celebrated on the 15th of August. Built mostly in Baroque style and built in 1656 on the plans of Tumas Dingli. The trek now closes at Gnien Raymond Caruana for a well deserved rest or kids play time.

highlights.title: Getting there
highlights.description: Route starts from the parish church of Gudja and ends at a nearby playground. Bus 88 from Valletta goes to start location. Most parking slots in tax-Xlejli area if by private transport. Start location coordinates: 35.8481865, 14.5027438

highlights.title: Type of Walk
highlights.description: Most of walk goes along countryside roads in Gudja and Ghaxaq to enjoy most of the nature views in this area.

highlights.title: Terrain
highlights.description: Very flat route, most of it on tarmac roads with parts on a bit bumpier concrete paths.

highlights.title: Safety Warning
highlights.description: While light on traffic, vehicles can approach on some of the narrow countryside roads. Keep caution by sticking to left side of the road.

highlights.title: Amenities
highlights.description: Route takes to a playground with restroom facilities a few minutes after the start, and ends in another public garden. Restaurant with Maltese cuisine near Parish church.
